unit.name: What people actually use today to evaluate LLMs
unit.description: Surveys current LLM evaluation practice across perplexity, aggregate benchmark suites, arena-style comparisons, and specialized tasks such as coding and agents.
unit.summary: Yann summarizes current LLM evaluation practice into three families: perplexity or validation loss, averages over many benchmark tasks, and arena-like pairwise comparisons [ts=3302s] [ts=3327s]. The 'average over everything' family includes large suites such as HELM and the Hugging Face Open LLM Leaderboard, which bundle automatically scored tasks including GSM8K, MMLU, LegalBench, and MedQA [ts=3370s] [ts=3395s]. MMLU is highlighted as the most widely believed benchmark, consisting of multiple-choice questions over 57 subject areas, while coding benchmarks are valued because they correlate with reasoning and support easy automatic evaluation via test cases [ts=3434s] [ts=3512s] [ts=3543s]. The segment also raises agent evaluation as an emerging challenge requiring sandboxed tool environments and notes that perplexity is often a surprisingly strong proxy for downstream averages in pretrained models, though it is not comparable across datasets or tokenizers and should be treated carefully [ts=3600s] [ts=3691s] [ts=3750s].

key_points.text: Current LLM evaluation commonly uses perplexity, average benchmark scores, or arena-like pairwise comparisons.
HELM and the Hugging Face Open LLM Leaderboard are aggregate suites over many automatically evaluated tasks.
Common benchmark tasks include GSM8K, MMLU, LegalBench, and MedQA.
MMLU is a 57-task multiple-choice benchmark that many people treat as a key indicator of general capability.
Coding is widely benchmarked because it correlates with reasoning and can be checked automatically with tests.
Agent benchmarks need sandboxed environments because evaluating real actions safely is difficult.
Perplexity on pretraining/validation data is often strongly correlated with average downstream benchmark performance.
Perplexity is not comparable across datasets or tokenizers.

The 'average over everything' family includes large suites such as HELM and the Hugging Face Open LLM Leaderboard, which bundle automatically scored tasks including GSM8K, MMLU, LegalBench, and MedQA [ts=3370s] [ts=3395s]. MMLU is highlighted as the most widely believed benchmark, consisting of multiple-choice questions over 57 subject areas, while coding benchmarks are valued because they correlate with reasoning and support easy automatic evaluation via test cases [ts=3434s] [ts=3512s] [ts=3543s]. The segment also raises agent evaluation as an emerging challenge requiring sandboxed tool environments and notes that perplexity is often a surprisingly strong proxy for downstream averages in pretrained models, though it is not comparable across datasets or tokenizers and should be treated carefully [ts=3600s] [ts=3691s] [ts=3750s].

kp_catalog.name: Current LLM benchmark suites and task coverage
kp_catalog.description: Modern LLM evaluation uses broad benchmark suites spanning math, knowledge, code, law, medicine, and other domains.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because current LLM benchmark suites and task coverage clarifies modern LLM evaluation uses broad benchmark suites spanning math, knowledge, code, law, medicine, and other domains, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Perplexity as a proxy metric
kp_catalog.description: Pretraining or validation perplexity often tracks downstream performance but is not universally comparable or sufficient.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because perplexity as a proxy metric clarifies pretraining or validation perplexity often tracks downstream performance but is not universally comparable or sufficient, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
